frameworks.name: Moral High Ground Delivery Change Pitch
frameworks.definition: A reframing technique for announcing service delivery reductions: position the change as fulfilling a prior promise (integrity), enumerate reasons it benefits clients, and make any objection equivalent to attacking your integrity.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a business is supply-constrained (cannot take more customers), the correct lever is delivery restructuring, not changing the selling or demand-generation model. Changing demand-side mechanics while supply is the binding constraint wastes resources and does not remove the actual growth ceiling.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Business has more demand than it can fulfill
Founder is the sole delivery resource
claims.mechanism: If sales are capped by delivery capacity, adding demand only deepens the backlog; only expanding or restructuring delivery capacity removes the ceiling.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the business is demand-constrained (not enough customers)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that founders over-deliver beyond their contractual promise primarily because of insecurity about whether clients will receive enough value, not because of genuine contractual obligation. This insecurity causes them to keep hopping on extra calls that were never part of the original agreement.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Early-stage coaching or consulting business
Founder is sole delivery resource
claims.mechanism: Founder insecurity about value delivery leads to offering access and responsiveness beyond what was contracted, inflating real delivery cost without changing the price or formal scope.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a coach or service provider responds quickly and answers client questions immediately, it trains clients to reach out more frequently, because rapid response functions as a positive reinforcement schedule. The founder's responsiveness directly creates the volume of inbound demands they experience.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: High-touch coaching or consulting relationship
Founder personally responds to all inquiries
claims.mechanism: Clients receive an immediate reward (answer, attention) for reaching out; this reinforcement increases the frequency of the reaching-out behavior in subsequent periods.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that clients' expectations of delivery are set by the service provider, not by the clients themselves. The provider who trains clients to expect high-touch access can retrain them to accept different touchpoints because expectation is a learned behavior, not an inherent right.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Service business with existing client relationships
Provider previously established over-delivery norms
claims.mechanism: Expectations are formed by prior consistent behavior from the provider; therefore, new consistent behavior from the provider resets expectations over time.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that founder direct accessibility (personal cell phone access, immediate response) is a genuine competitive differentiator in the early underdog stage of a service business, but becomes a delivery liability once the business has proven its credibility. The narrative must flip: early stage is 'you get me, they don't'; established stage is 'I've proven value, you don't need my cell.'
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Early stage: business has not yet established market credibility
Established stage: business has proven client results and market standing
claims.mechanism: In underdog stage, high-touch access compensates for lack of social proof and brand; at established stage, the value is already demonstrated, so the access is no longer needed as a differentiator and instead creates a scaling ceiling.
bounded_by: Does not apply to self-serve or productized digital businesses where founder access is never the differentiator

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that announcing a reduction in service delivery should never be positioned as 'you're getting less,' but rather as the provider honoring a higher obligation. The framing that works is: 'I made a promise and have realized I am breaking it; to stay in integrity, I must change this to the following, which benefits you in these ways.' This framing makes any objection to the change equivalent to the client accusing the provider of being dishonest.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Service business reducing delivery scope for existing clients
Provider previously over-delivered relative to contractual promise
claims.mechanism: Framing the change as an integrity issue inverts the social dynamic: the client cannot object without implicitly calling the provider dishonest, which makes objections socially costly and therefore rare.
bounded_by: Requires that the provider genuinely has a mission or prior promise they can reference
Fails if clients have independent communication channels and can coordinate a collective objection

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a service provider reduces delivery scope for existing clients, they will lose some clients, and that is acceptable because the vacated slots will be filled by new clients who pay more and arrive without the prior expectations of over-delivery.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Service business moving from high-touch over-delivery to scaled delivery model
Business currently has demand it cannot fulfill
claims.mechanism: Clients who accepted terms based on over-delivery norms will churn when those norms change; new clients who never experienced over-delivery will not expect it, resetting the service contract at a higher price with lower delivery cost.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that continuing a delivery model that the founder personally hates and cannot sustain is incompatible with a stated mission to serve more people, because founder burnout eventually collapses both the business and the mission. The two choices are: reduce ambition and stay small, or change delivery so the mission can continue.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Founder is sole delivery resource running at or near maximum capacity
Founder has stated mission to serve a growing number of clients
claims.mechanism: A founder-dependent delivery model caps output at the founder's personal bandwidth; sustained over-capacity operation leads to burnout and eventual business failure, permanently preventing the mission from being fulfilled.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that when a coaching business founder is already supply-constrained, the correct path to scale is to either hire a second delivery person or restructure the delivery format to serve more clients per unit of founder time, not to modify demand-generation channels or selling cadence.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Coaching or consulting business
Founder is the only delivery resource
Demand exceeds current delivery capacity

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a delivery system where a single founder runs 90-minute weekly sessions for six active cohorts plus daily office hours represents a high-touch model that is not inherently wrong, but is capped at that founder's available hours. The problem is not the cohort structure per se but the exclusive founder dependency within it.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Cohort-based coaching model
Founder is only coach

anchors.region: six active cohorts at 90 min/week each
daily office hours plus other Zoom calls

examples.text: A pet care coaching business (Dogco Launch, founded by an unnamed founder) was in its third year, having grown to $356k in annual revenue in the prior year and projecting $1.2M for the current year (2025) and $3M for 2026. The founder ran a scarcity-based cohort model with 20-25 companies per cohort, launching twice a year, and was personally conducting 90-minute weekly coaching sessions with each of six active cohorts plus daily office hours, totaling approximately 45 hours per week of Zoom calls. Despite having demand, the founder could not take on more customers because all delivery capacity was consumed. Alex Hormozi diagnosed this as a supply constraint, not a demand constraint, and advised restructuring delivery norms rather than changing the selling model.

The Dogco Launch founder had established a norm of high accessibility with clients — personal cell phone access, rapid responses to any question — which was originally positioned as a differentiator over larger competitors who treated clients as numbers. By the time the business had multiple active cohorts, this norm had trained clients to expect immediate access and had inflated delivery time well beyond what was formally promised, creating a cycle where the founder feared cutting back access because client expectations had been set so high. Alex Hormozi identified this as a case where the founder's own responsiveness behavior had trained the client base to demand more, and that retraining the clients required changing the reinforcement schedule, not capitulating to the existing one.

Alex Hormozi described his own past practice of announcing delivery changes to existing customers by using an integrity-based framing. Rather than saying 'we are giving you less,' the announcement was structured as: 'I made a promise to you. I have realized recently that I am actually breaking this promise. So I no longer can stay out of integrity with what I promised you. In an effort to stick with my integrity, I have to change it to this — and here are 17 reasons why this is better for you.' He noted that after this framing, if a client objected, the provider could respond: 'Are you telling me to question my integrity?' which neutralized the objection.

practices.text: When clients ask for your time outside of agreed delivery sessions, route them to an available resource (FAQ, document, recording) or to the next scheduled calendar slot rather than answering immediately, to avoid reinforcing the behavior of unscheduled outreach.

When announcing a delivery model change to existing clients, enumerate at least several concrete reasons why the new model benefits them — new investments you can make in their outcomes, previously unmet promises you can now fulfill — rather than simply describing what will no longer be provided.

Before changing demand-generation activities (content, selling cadence, channels), first diagnose whether the business is supply-constrained or demand-constrained; only modify demand-side inputs when a genuine demand constraint exists.
